SUMMARY:

FULL CONTENT:
# Health Systems Strengthening

Short Courses

Face-to-face

5 ECTS Credits

English

28/04/25
-

16/05/25

## General information

The aim of this 3-week course is to enable students to seize opportunities for health system strengthening – provided by national policies, specific disease-control programmes, interventions of international agencies or global health initiatives – in order to contribute to universal health coverage.

The need for managers of health programmes and (local) health systems, and policymakers capable to develop and strengthen their (local) health systems in an effective and comprehensive way remains huge. Shocks like the COVID-19 pandemic once again demonstrated the importance of strong and resilient health services and systems. The climate crisis is just another reason why we need to invest in resilient and sustainable health systems.

We adopt WHO’s definition of a ‘health system’ as all organisations, people and actions whose primary intent is to promote, restore or maintain health. We use the term ‘health system strengthening’ (HSS), but we define this as to include health system development (HSD). In short, the term HSS is often used in the literature to indicate the policies of external agencies to strengthen health systems, while HSD refers to domestic efforts to develop and improve health systems. Yet, the underlying principles and processes are similar, given that both deal with the health system and its structural determinants of performance. We thus frame health system strengthening within system-wide reforms and policies at national and global level. We include perspectives from different disciplines such as health policy and systems research, change management, complexity and systems thinking, and political science.

The course draws on ITM’s comprehensive expertise and field experience in the development, implementation and evaluation of health systems strengthening strategies in a wide range of low- and middle-income resource settings. The input of ITM staff is complemented by the involvement of partners and alumni, who will present country-based case studies. Participants also benefit from the rich exchange of experience between professionals originating from different parts of the world – where possible, we will draw upon participants’ experience for discussions of specific country cases.

## Learning objectives

At the end of this course, the participant should be able to effectively contribute to the development, implementation and assessment of strategies for health system strengthening. More specifically, the participants should be able to:

FULL CONTENT:
![Home](/ResourcePackages/WHO/assets/dist/images/logos/en/h-logo-blue.svg)

# Strengthening health systems through neglected tropical disease (NTD) integration: country experiences and innovative approaches

You are invited to join a Webinar hosted by the **Global Onchocerciasis Network for Elimination (GONE)** Strengthening Health Systems Through Neglected Tropical Disease (NTD) Integration: Country Experiences and Innovative approaches.

## Objective of the webinar

This webinar brings together countries and stakeholders to share experiences and insights on integrating Neglected Tropical Disease (NTD) programs into broader national health systems. Panelists and attendees will examine practical approaches for incorporating NTD interventions into existing initiatives such as primary health care, malaria control, child health days, and immunization campaigns, while making efficient use of available infrastructure to improve impact and sustainability, optimize resources, streamline efforts, and maintain consistent delivery of NTD services. The event will gather representatives from Ministries of Health, implementing organizations, policymakers, researchers, and public health professionals to discuss integration strategies in the context of a changing global health environment. By exchanging real-world examples and practical solutions, the discussion aims to produce actionable recommendations.

[**Register here**](https://who.zoom.us/webinar/register/WN_-UaEhIxMSu2s6eKPntKpYQ) to participate in the webinar

--------------------------------------

## Agenda

|  |
| --- |
| **Introductions: Navigating Global Health Transitions - Advancing NTD Integration** *Dr Maria Rebollo,* WHO |
| ****Strengthening Integrated Approaches to NTDs: Lessons from Rwanda and Togo**** *Mr Ladislas Nshimiyimana,* Rwanda Biomedical Centre/Ministry of Health of Rwanda*Dr Piham Gnossike,* Ministry of Health of Togo |
| ****Integration of NTD interventions into primary health care services in Ethiopia, Kenya and Uganda**** *Mr Tesfahun Bishaw,* Ministry of Health of Ethiopia*Mr Wycliff Omondi,* Ministry of Health of Kenya *Dr Alfred Mubangizi,* Ministry of Health of Uganda |
| ****Embedding NTD Interventions Within Malaria Programs and Vaccination Campaigns in Madagascar and Tanzania**** *Dr Aina Faes Tolotra,* Ministry of Health of Madagascar*Dr Clarer Jones,* Ministry of Health of Tanzania |
| ****Incorporating NTD Programs into Child Health Days in Benin and Niger**** *Dr Ndeye-***Marie Adama Bassabi-Alladji,** Ministry of Health of Benin  *Dr Salissou Adamou Batchiri,* Ministry of Health of Niger |
| **Discussion** |
| **Wrap-up and conclusions** *Dr Carol Karutu,* The END Fund |

Languages: English and French

enriched_points.expanded_content: Enhancing healthcare systems is crucial for improving service delivery, resilience, and access to care, ensuring effective responses to health crises and integrating services for tropical diseases.

Active community involvement fosters trust, improves health literacy, and empowers populations to participate in health initiatives, essential for sustainable public health outcomes in tropical medicine.

Combining educational programs with health initiatives promotes awareness, prevention, and early intervention, ultimately leading to improved health outcomes and reduced disease prevalence in tropical regions.

Recognizing and addressing social determinants, such as poverty and education, is vital for equitable health access and outcomes, particularly in resource-limited settings affected by tropical diseases.

Investing in research and innovative solutions enhances disease understanding, treatment options, and preventive measures, driving progress in managing tropical medicine challenges and improving public health.
